trigger: David Sacks tweets about AI bailout
quote: If OpenAI fails, Google can readily step in, no bailout required.
claim: If OpenAI fails, Google is capable of stepping in to fill the gap, making a government bailout of AI unnecessary.

quote: the White House's new Science and AI program, Genesis, announced by Executive Order on Monday, does seem to involve the government buying a lot of chips from a lot of AI companies, many of which are losing money.
claim: The White House Genesis program involves government purchases of chips from AI companies that are losing money, effectively functioning as a subsidy or bailout.

quote: it will be interesting to see both whether all these LLMs have a genuine, positive impact on science (or just produce a lot of queries that go nowhere, a digital version of the infamous never-built Gravina Island Bridge) and whether this is the last government subsidy of the AI industry, or just the first of many.
claim: Government-funded LLM applications in science may produce little genuine impact, and the Genesis program may be the first of many government subsidies for the AI industry.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: The thing which I think is the most fundamental is that these models somehow just generalize dramatically worse than people. And it's super obvious. That seems like a very fundamental thing.
claim: LLMs generalize dramatically worse than humans, and this is a fundamental limitation of the technology.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: AGI will never emerge straight from LLMs, and that to a certain extent that they have run their course, and that we do in fact need new ideas.
claim: AGI will never emerge from LLMs alone; the technology has largely run its course and new approaches are needed.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: The first and most obvious answer is money, which I estimate, back of the envelope as (roughly) a trillion dollars, much of it on Nvidia chips and massive salaries.
claim: Approximately a trillion dollars has been spent on the LLM scaling approach, much of it on Nvidia chips and massive salaries.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: If the definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results, trillion dollar investments in ever more expensive experiments aiming to reach AGI may be delusional to the highest degree.
claim: Continued trillion-dollar investments in scaling LLMs to reach AGI are delusional given the consistent failure to solve core problems.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: To a first approximation, all the big tech companies, from OpenAI to Google to Meta to xAI to Anthropic to several Chinese companies, keep doing the same experiment over and over: building ever larger LLMs in hopes of reaching AGI.
claim: All major AI companies are repeating the same experiment of building ever-larger LLMs hoping to reach AGI, despite consistent failure to solve core issues.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: none of these experiments has solved core issues around hallucinations, generalization, planning and reasoning
claim: No amount of LLM scaling has solved the core issues of hallucinations, generalization, planning, and reasoning.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: the world has gone 'all in' on LLMs, but, as Sutskever's interview highlights, there are many reasons to doubt that LLMs will ever deliver the rewards that many people expected.
claim: The world has gone all in on LLMs, but there are many reasons to doubt LLMs will ever deliver the rewards people expected.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: the machine learning community has arrogantly excluded other voices, and indeed whole other fields like the cognitive sciences. And we all now may be about to pay the price.
claim: The machine learning community's arrogant exclusion of cognitive science and other fields has contributed to the current AI bubble and its potential economic fallout.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: we may have wasted a trillion dollars and several years to rediscover what cognitive science already knew
claim: The AI industry may have wasted a trillion dollars and several years rediscovering what cognitive science already knew about the limits of pattern-matching approaches.

trigger: Ilya Sutskever interview on scaling limits
quote: the impact of a deflated AI bubble could be immense
claim: The deflation of the AI bubble could have immense economic impact, potentially causing a recession.

quote: I think a fair case can be made that it is not what it has often been cracked up to be, and probably never will be.
claim: ChatGPT is not what it has been hyped to be and probably never will be.

quote: I just don't happen to think large language models like ChatGPT will get us there.
claim: Large language models like ChatGPT will not achieve artificial general intelligence.

quote: I think that the recurring core technical problems that we have seen (as discussed below) with LLMs aren't going way; instead they inherent to the technology.
claim: The recurring core technical problems of LLMs (hallucinations, unreliable reasoning, alignment issues) are inherent to the technology and will not go away.

quote: AGI (AI with the breadth and resourcesfulness of human intelligence) is not coming soon.
claim: AGI is not coming soon.

quote: productivity studies sometimes show a 30% gain, but none have come close to 10x (1000% gain)
claim: AI productivity gains for workers are around 30% at best, nowhere near the 10x gains that were promised.

quote: The results are disappointing because the underlying tech is unreliable, And that's been obvious from the start.
claim: The disappointing results of generative AI adoption stem from the underlying technology being unreliable.

quote: Three years and nearly a trillion dollars later almost of all it remains true.
claim: The core limitations Marcus identified in December 2022 (unreliability, hallucinations, poor reasoning, alignment problems) remain true three years and nearly a trillion dollars later.

quote: the same problems have continued to plague every single model since—from GPT 4.0 to GPT 4.1 to GPT 4.5 and GPT-5 (and many others) to all the variations on Claude, all the variations on Gemini, all the variations on Grok, all the variations on llama, all the variations on DeepSeek, and so on.
claim: The same core problems (hallucinations, unreliable reasoning, alignment issues) have plagued every LLM model from every company, including GPT variants, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Llama, and DeepSeek.

quote: Hundreds of models, always the same failure modes.
claim: Across hundreds of LLM models, the same failure modes persist.

quote: LLMs have undoubtedly gotten quantitively better, but qualitively the core problems remain.
claim: LLMs have improved quantitatively but qualitatively the core problems of hallucination, unreliable reasoning, and poor generalization remain.

quote: deep learning (the tech underling large language models) is a better ladder; but a better ladder doesn't necessarily get you to the moon.
claim: Deep learning / LLMs represent incremental improvement that cannot reach the goal of AGI, like a better ladder that cannot get you to the moon.

quote: My verdict, in short, is that ChatGPT is a trillion dollar experiment that has failed.
claim: ChatGPT is a trillion-dollar experiment that has failed to solve the core cognitive problems.

quote: ChatGPT has failed to make profits for companies like OpenAI.
claim: ChatGPT has failed to make profits for OpenAI due to the discrepancy between reliability and the immense costs of internet-scale data dependence.

quote: Ultimately though, what LLMs are great at seeming is plausible – while being much less good at getting their facts straight.
claim: LLMs excel at seeming plausible but are poor at factual accuracy.

quote: The gulf between how impressive something *seems* and how useful something *is* has never been greater.
claim: The gap between how impressive LLMs seem in demos and how useful they actually are is historically large.

quote: ChatGPT obviously lacks the reliability of calculators (which is something the field should aspire to)
claim: ChatGPT lacks the reliability of calculators, and the field should aspire to calculator-level reliability.

quote: any normal seven-year old develops a rich model of the world, including the objects they see, the people they interact with and so on, and even how their bodies work. GPT is still just faking it.
claim: Even a normal seven-year-old develops a richer model of the world than GPT, which is still faking understanding.

quote: ChatGPT has never grown up, in the sense of addressing the core challenges that I have set out. And on its own (without the aid of neurosymbolic systems and world models) probably never will.
claim: ChatGPT will probably never address its core challenges without the aid of neurosymbolic systems and world models.

quote: large language models have become a commodity; LLM profits outside of Nvidia are hard to find
claim: Large language models have become a commodity, and profits from LLMs outside of Nvidia are hard to find.

quote: the reality is OpenAI has ridden to a $500 billion valuation on hopes, not profits. And those hopes are slipping away, as usage declines and others catch up.
claim: OpenAI's $500 billion valuation is based on hopes rather than profits, and those hopes are slipping as usage declines and competitors catch up.

quote: In the end LLMs will continue to exist, no matter what; they will get cheaper. And they will find some utility (indeed they are already have). But they will never be AGI, and they never be much more than commodity.
claim: LLMs will continue to exist and get cheaper, finding some utility, but will never achieve AGI and will never be more than a commodity.

quote: The natural home for ChatGPT turns out to be… demos.
claim: ChatGPT's natural strength is in demos and first impressions rather than reliable production use.

quote: Amateurs can in fact now build prototypes, but the auto-generated code tends to be shaky and few amateurs can make those prototypes robust.
claim: LLMs enable amateurs to build prototypes, but the auto-generated code is shaky and few amateurs can make those prototypes robust.

quote: Anyone assuming that everything is simultaneously rising exponentially, and that it will continue to do so indefinitely, is a fool.
claim: The assumption that AI progress is rising exponentially on all fronts and will continue indefinitely is incorrect; different benchmarks show different patterns of progress.

quote: if Sustekver and I (and at this point many others) are right that scaling is largely played out, a decline in demand is sure to follow
claim: If scaling is largely played out, a decline in demand for AI compute is sure to follow.

quote: GenAI is like a rerun of that situation, an order or two of magnitude more hype, coupled with equal naïveté about what a cognitively adequate solution would actually look like, only this time with an extra decimal place, potentially a trillion dollars down the drain
claim: Generative AI repeats the pattern of driverless car hype but at one or two orders of magnitude greater scale, potentially wasting a trillion dollars.

quote: For the love of Darwin, please let's spend the next eight years considering new approaches, not tanking the economy on more of the same.
claim: The AI field should spend the next eight years pursuing new approaches rather than continuing to scale LLMs.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: OpenAI has clearly squandered the sizable lead they once had.
claim: OpenAI has squandered the sizable technical lead it once had over competitors.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: My guess is that they have cumulatively raised on the order of $100 billion since they launched, perhaps more than any other company in history, but have already spent most of it, and likely don't have much more than a year's runway left.
claim: OpenAI has raised approximately $100 billion but spent most of it and likely has no more than a year of runway remaining.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: Since they are losing money and can't stand on their own two feet for long. That puts them at the mercy of investors.
claim: OpenAI cannot sustain itself financially and is at the mercy of investors for survival.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: If things continue to deteriorate, raising the next round will be a lot harder than the last.
claim: If OpenAI's position continues to deteriorate, its next fundraising round will be significantly harder than previous rounds.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: If OpenAI drops, Nvidia might well also drop.
claim: A significant decline in OpenAI's valuation could cause Nvidia's stock to drop as well.

trigger: OpenAI declares Code Red over Google Gemini
quote: In the worse case, the government might seriously consider a bailout a taxpayer expense.
claim: In the worst case, the US government might seriously consider bailing out AI companies at taxpayer expense.

trigger: Trump allows Nvidia H200 sales to China
quote: maybe, just maybe, China's reticence to stock up on H200s is also a sign that China has realized that GPU's aren't the royal road to AGI
claim: China's reluctance to buy H200 chips may indicate that China has recognized that GPUs and scaling are not the path to AGI.

trigger: Trump allows Nvidia H200 sales to China
quote: loading up on AI infrastructure that is likely to rapidly depreciate might be premature — and not the massive competitive or economic advantage many once people thought it was.
claim: Loading up on AI infrastructure (GPUs, data centers) is premature because it will rapidly depreciate and is not the competitive advantage many assumed.

trigger: Trump allows Nvidia H200 sales to China
quote: The first country to really appreciate all this may get a huge competitive advantage, in whatever comes next, after LLMs.
claim: The first country to recognize the limits of LLMs and pivot to what comes next will gain a huge competitive advantage.

trigger: Trump AI deregulation executive order and H200 sales to China
quote: The only excuse I can see for the White House's escalating efforts at leaving AI all but entirely deregulated (unlikely virtually any other industry from medicine to food to airplanes to cars to constructions) is some sort of race with China.
claim: The White House's push to leave AI almost entirely unregulated is unprecedented compared to every other industry and is only justifiable if premised on a race with China.

trigger: Trump AI deregulation executive order and H200 sales to China
quote: If things go wrong – whether that means the bubble bursting (with possible harm to the economy), or China beats the US in the LLM race, or some massive AI-caused disaster such as a giant cyberattack — Trump (and Sacks) will own the mess.
claim: If the AI bubble bursts, China wins the AI race, or a major AI-caused disaster occurs, Trump and Sacks will bear responsibility due to their deregulation policies.

trigger: Trump AI deregulation executive order and H200 sales to China
quote: By the end of the week (when the Executive Order is expected), American citizens will have almost no route to protection from the consequences of AI
claim: After Trump's executive order preempting state AI laws, American citizens will have almost no legal protection from the consequences of AI.

trigger: Trump signs executive order blocking state AI regulation
quote: My own hunch is that all this is going to blow up on Trump (or at least on his heir apparent J.D. Vance) badly.
claim: Trump's AI deregulation policy will backfire politically on Trump or J.D. Vance.

trigger: Trump signs executive order blocking state AI regulation
quote: Anything that goes wrong, from AI-fueled cybercrime to bioweapons attacks facilitated by AI to teen suicides apparently linked to GenAI will be on his hands, and his reputation.
claim: Trump will bear political responsibility for any harms from AI including cybercrime, bioweapons facilitated by AI, and teen suicides linked to GenAI, due to his deregulation.

trigger: Trump signs executive order blocking state AI regulation
quote: he will also be closely tied to any AI-tinged economic debacle that happens on his watch.
claim: Trump will be politically tied to any AI-related economic downturn that occurs during his administration.

trigger: Trump signs executive order blocking state AI regulation
quote: The opposite of overregulation is underregulation; here we now essentially have zero regulation. What we obviously need is something in between, fostering innovation but also protecting our citizens.
claim: The US needs AI regulation that balances innovation with citizen protection, rather than the current state of essentially zero regulation.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: the generative AI race is not—and will not ever be—'winner take all', any more than Coke's long standing battle with Pepsi has been winner-take-all.
claim: The generative AI race between the US and China is not and will never be winner-take-all; it will be more like Coke vs Pepsi.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: I literally don't see any plausible scenario in which either nation outright 'wins' the generative AI race to the exclusion of the other.
claim: There is no plausible scenario in which either the US or China outright wins the generative AI race to the exclusion of the other.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: since everybody is following the same playbook—ever more massive data, ever more massive servers, fed into ever-larger large language models—nobody has a real technical advantage here.
claim: No country has a real technical advantage in generative AI because everyone is following the same playbook of scaling LLMs with more data and compute.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: Right now, as it happens, China is at most a few months behind; I expect the lead to be slight and to flip back and forth, with nobody retaining a sizeable a lead for long.
claim: China is at most a few months behind the US in generative AI, and the lead will flip back and forth with neither side retaining a sizable lead.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: genAI models are, too. Building our AI policy around a fantasy that we are somehow going to crush China in LLM war (or vice versa!) is misguided.
claim: Generative AI models are commodities, and building US AI policy around the fantasy of crushing China in an LLM war is misguided.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: it may be that the real winner is whichever country doesn't overextend itself to the point of financial ruin, in a foolish effort to win a race that can't be won.
claim: In the AI race, the real winner may be whichever country avoids overextending itself financially rather than the one that spends the most.

trigger: Trump executive order on AI and China competition claims
quote: All the more so if LLMs turn out to be a dud, or if LLMs are replaced by smaller, more efficient systems that don't demand such immense amounts of infrastructure.
claim: The risk of overinvestment in AI infrastructure is even greater if LLMs turn out to be a dud or are replaced by smaller, more efficient systems.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: The problem with generative AI has always been that large language models associate patterns together without really understanding those patterns; it's statistics without comprehension.
claim: LLMs associate patterns without understanding them; they perform statistics without comprehension.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: A lot of hallucinations are borne of exactly this kind of overgeneralization
claim: Many LLM hallucinations are caused by overgeneralization from spurious statistical correlations between words rather than genuine conceptual understanding.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: it's not even that LLMs are picking up on real correlations in the world ... it's that the LLMs learn weird nth order correlations between words (rather than concepts)
claim: LLMs learn nth-order correlations between words rather than between real-world concepts, leading to bizarre and incorrect associations.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: There is no way in Darwin's green earth that we are ever going to be able to patch what is likely to be an endless list of vulnerabilities.
claim: The security vulnerabilities of LLMs stemming from statistical correlation are effectively unpatchable due to being an endless list.

trigger: Owain Evans research on LLM vulnerabilities
quote: Putting society in the hands of giant, superficial correlation machines is not going to end well.
claim: Relying on LLMs (superficial correlation machines) for important societal functions will end badly.

quote: One of the costliest mistakes in scientific history is coming to an end
claim: The belief that scaling LLMs would lead to AGI is one of the costliest mistakes in scientific history, and it is now coming to an end.

quote: these systems would never be reliable enough, and that even with more data they would have trouble with hallucinations, factuality, reasoning, outliers, and generalization.
claim: LLMs will never be reliable enough, even with more data, and will continue to have problems with hallucinations, factuality, reasoning, outliers, and generalization.

quote: The return on investment that people dream of is just not there, for most customers.
claim: The return on investment from generative AI is not materializing for most business customers.

quote: If we are to build AGI, we are going to need to learn something from humans, how they reason and understand the physical world, and how they represent and acquire language and complex concepts.
claim: Building AGI requires learning from human cognition -- how humans reason, understand the physical world, and represent and acquire language and complex concepts.

quote: Better late than never. Let's bring in the cognitive scientists, and stop fantasizing that data and compute will solve all our problems. The time for neurosymbolic AI and world models and causality is now.
claim: The AI field needs to bring in cognitive scientists and shift to neurosymbolic AI, world models, and causality research rather than continuing to rely on data and compute scaling.

quote: We won't get to AGI in 2026 (or 7).
claim: AGI will not be achieved in 2026 or 2027.

quote: Human domestic robots like Optimus and Figure will be all demo and very little product.
claim: Human domestic robots like Tesla Optimus and Figure will be mostly demos with very little actual product in 2026.

quote: No country will take a decisive lead in the GenAI 'race'.
claim: No country will take a decisive lead in the generative AI race in 2026.

quote: Work on new approaches such as world models and neurosymbolic will escalate.
claim: Work on new AI approaches such as world models and neurosymbolic AI will escalate in 2026.

quote: 2025 will be known as the year of the peak bubble, and also the moment at which Wall Street began to lose confidence in generative AI.
claim: 2025 will be known as the year of the peak AI bubble and the moment Wall Street began to lose confidence in generative AI.

quote: Valuations may go up before they fall, but the Oracle craze early in September and what has happened since will in hindsight be seen as the beginning of the end.
claim: AI valuations may temporarily rise but will ultimately fall; the Oracle craze in September 2025 will be seen as the beginning of the end.

quote: Backlash to Generative AI and radical deregulation will escalate. In the midterms, AI will be an election issue for first time. Trump may eventually distance himself from AI because of this backlash.
claim: Backlash to generative AI and radical deregulation will escalate, AI will become an election issue in the midterms for the first time, and Trump may distance himself from AI.

quote: coding tools sometimes impair productivity rather than increasing it
claim: AI coding tools sometimes impair programmer productivity rather than increasing it.

quote: We could easily wind up with too many data centers, and a lot of chips that rapidly lose their value.
claim: The US could easily end up with excess data centers and rapidly depreciating AI chips.

quote: In the worst case, as I have warned before, we may hear shouts of 'too big to fail' and calls for government bailouts.
claim: In the worst case, the AI industry downturn will lead to 'too big to fail' arguments and calls for government bailouts.

quote: And, honestly, I think that is the most likely scenario.
claim: The most likely scenario for the AI industry involves calls for government bailouts.

quote: Going all in on Generative AI, without diversifying intellectually and economically, is likely an immense mistake.
claim: Going all in on generative AI without intellectual and economic diversification is likely an immense mistake.

quote: If September 2025 was peak bubble, 2026 will likely the year which it all falls apart.
claim: September 2025 was the peak of the AI bubble, and 2026 will likely be the year it all falls apart.

quote: the economics don't make sense, and never will, in large part because of the core technical problems that I have stressed repeatedly here and elsewhere since 2019.
claim: The economics of LLMs do not make sense and never will because of inherent core technical problems.

quote: Without world models. you cannot achieve reliability. And without reliability, profits are limited.
claim: Without world models, AI systems cannot achieve reliability, and without reliability, profits from AI will be limited.

quote: The technical problems are not new. And a trillion dollars or so of investment hasn't remedied them.
claim: A trillion dollars of investment has not remedied the core technical problems of LLMs.

quote: they have at last become widely recognized, not just as the transitory 'bugs' the industry wanted you to think of, but as inherent limitations that flow from the very design of LLMs they really are.
claim: LLM problems like hallucinations and unreliable reasoning are now widely recognized as inherent design limitations rather than transitory bugs.

quote: Once the implications are fully appreciated, the whole thing will begin to unwind.
claim: Once the full implications of LLMs' inherent limitations are appreciated, the AI bubble will begin to unwind.

quote: the dominance of LLMs may soon start to decline — and we may start seeing newer, more sound approaches start to emerge.
claim: The dominance of LLMs may soon start to decline and newer, more sound AI approaches will start to emerge.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: I too have huge doubts about LLMs.
claim: Marcus has huge doubts that LLMs will lead to AGI or solve their core reliability problems.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: A world model (if my 2020 article is correct) needs to be full of explicit, structured, directly retrievable knowledge about time, space, causality, people, places, objects, events, and so on. You need all that for formal reasoning. And you need all that to avoid hallucinations.
claim: World models need explicit, structured, directly retrievable knowledge about time, space, causality, people, places, objects, and events to enable formal reasoning and avoid hallucinations.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: LeCun, who knows neural networks but refuses to engage in discussion about what classical AI brings to the table, doesn't really see that. My own view, which I stand by, is that we need 'neurosymbolic' contributions from both traditions if we are to get to trustworthy AI.
claim: Achieving trustworthy AI requires neurosymbolic contributions from both neural network and classical AI traditions.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: what LeCun has wound up with for 'world models' is just another opaque, uninterpretable neural network that doesn't lend itself to reasoning.
claim: LeCun's JEPA approach to world models is just another opaque neural network that does not lend itself to reasoning.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: I don't expect it to solve many of the problems that have been plaguing LLMs.
claim: LeCun's JEPA-based world model approach will not solve many of the problems plaguing LLMs.

trigger: Yann LeCun leaves Meta to start new company
quote: He has the right name for a thing we need ('world model') but an inadequate implementation.
claim: LeCun has correctly identified world models as needed but has an inadequate neural-network-only implementation of them.

quote: By the end of 2026, President Trump will have begun to distance himself from the aggressively pro-AI industry policies that characterized his AI strategy in 2025.
claim: By the end of 2026, Trump will begin distancing himself from his aggressively pro-AI industry policies.

quote: The giant AI infrastructure plays (like Project Stargate) that he championed after his inauguration will look like an unprofitable and underused mistake.
claim: Giant AI infrastructure projects like Project Stargate will look like unprofitable and underused mistakes.

quote: So will his utter failure to meaningfully regulate AI, against the will of voters and political leaders both left and right.
claim: Trump's failure to meaningfully regulate AI will be seen as a mistake by end of 2026.

quote: Generative AI, once Silicon Valley's golden child, will start to look like a fad, a solution in search of a problem with economics that don't add up.
claim: Generative AI will start to look like a fad with economics that do not add up.

quote: Current AI] systems are patient, amoral, and fantastic at mimicry, making them among the greatest tools in history for generating mis- and disinformation
claim: Current AI systems are among the greatest tools in history for generating misinformation and disinformation due to their patience, amorality, and mimicry abilities.

quote: This, rather than some intelligence breakthrough, may well be the legacy of generative AI.
claim: The legacy of generative AI may be its use as a tool for disinformation rather than any intelligence breakthrough.

quote: the fog of war may become more terrifying as citizens lose trust in much of what they read or see, and when conflicts are started and escalated by false pretexts.
claim: AI-generated disinformation will make the fog of war more terrifying, erode citizen trust, and enable conflicts to be started or escalated by false pretexts.

trigger: WSJ puff piece on LeCun startup launch
quote: LeCun has not been right about everything, or even consistent in his own views over time, particularly with respect to LLMs
claim: Yann LeCun has not been right about everything in AI and has been inconsistent in his views over time, particularly regarding LLMs.

trigger: WSJ puff piece on LeCun startup launch
quote: he originated exactly none of these ideas, and (b) he rarely if ever credits any of the people who actually did
claim: LeCun originated none of his five most publicly associated ideas (CNNs, LLM critique, scaling critique, commonsense reasoning, world models) and rarely credits those who did.

trigger: WSJ puff piece on LeCun startup launch
quote: Whether he can produce genuinely original ideas in his new startup remains to be seen.
claim: It is uncertain whether LeCun can produce genuinely original ideas in his new startup given his history of appropriating others' ideas.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: Pure LLMs are inevitably stuck in the past, tied to when they are trained, and deeply limited in their inherent abilities to reason, search the web, 'think' critically etc.
claim: Pure LLMs are inevitably stuck in the past, tied to their training data, and deeply limited in reasoning, web search, and critical thinking abilities.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: The unreliability of LLMs in the face of novelty is one of the core reasons why businesses shouldn't trust LLMs
claim: LLMs' unreliability when faced with novel information is a core reason businesses should not trust them.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: they emanate from the same dynamics as hallucinations: pure LLMs correlate words in their training, but lack stable, revisable world models. Everything else is glommed on top, with more or less reliabilty.
claim: LLM failures on breaking news emanate from the same dynamics as hallucinations: correlating words without stable, revisable world models.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: So they forever play a game of catchup. Using massive numbers of humans (exact numbers unknown), GenAI companies perpetually put band-aids that they never disclose on top of their LLMs, based on human corrections, fixing each new problem as it arises. But the cycle repeats, endlessly.
claim: GenAI companies perpetually apply undisclosed human-correction band-aids on top of their LLMs, playing an endless game of catch-up that never resolves the underlying issues.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: the idea of using them to plot strategy in rapidly-changing environments like war is laughable.
claim: Using LLMs for military strategy in rapidly-changing environments is laughable given their inability to keep up with breaking news.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: as if either side had any chance of completely dominating GenAI
claim: Neither the US nor China has any chance of completely dominating generative AI.

trigger: US removal of Maduro from Venezuela
quote: the far more likely outcome is a tie
claim: The most likely outcome of the US-China generative AI race is a tie.
